weather_context.conditions: Cloudy night with high humidity and cold temperatures; no recent precipitation; light westerly wind.
weather_context.weather_impact_analysis: Relative humidity near 92% under full cloud cover will prolong surface moisture on ears and silks, which favors slow-growing storage/ear molds like Penicillium; cool temperatures (~1.3Â°C) slow many field fungi but Penicillium can persist and sporulate at lower temperatures, especially on previously wetted or damaged kernels. Lack of recent rain reduces splash-driven bacterial spread; wind is modest and less important for tip-localized molds. Overall, conditions support persistence and progression of Penicillium-type ear rot if grain is not dried promptly Document 1; Document 2.

image_description.plant_condition: Mature ear with dense blue-green, fuzzy/powdery mold at the tip; several tip kernels are discolored, shriveled, and partially decayed; lower kernels appear well-filled and sound.
image_description.environment: Ear appears exposed at the tip (husk not fully closed), consistent with moisture retention and mold development; no foliar symptoms visible.

discoloration.description: Blue-green fungal growth covering upper kernels and silk area with white patches and brown necrotic kernel tips.

characteristics.distribution: concentrated at ear tip with slight downward spread

surface_changes.description: Extensive powdery/fuzzy mold sporulation on exposed tip tissue and silks.

necrosis.location: upper 15â25% of ear (tip kernels and silks)
necrosis.extent: moderate and localized to the exposed tip

disease_name.scientific_name: Penicillium spp. (e.g., P. oxalicum, P. verrucosum, P. citrinum, P. crustosum)

key_diagnostic_features: Distinct blue-green, powdery/fuzzy mold colonizing the ear tip and adjacent kernels, a classic presentation of Penicillium ear rot Document 1; Document 2.
Tip-localized infection associated with exposed silks/kernels and damaged tissue; kernels at the tip are shriveled and necrotic Document 1; Document 2.
Often develops or progresses under cool, humid conditions and can continue post-harvest if grain is not dried and cooled promptly Document 1; Document 3.

differential_diagnoses.disease_name: Aspergillus ear rot (Aspergillus flavus, A. niger)
differential_diagnoses.likelihood: LowâAspergillus typically appears yellow-green (A. flavus) or black (A. niger) and is favored by hot, dry-to-warm conditions; the blue-green color under cool, humid weather points to Penicillium Document 1; Document 2.
LowâFusarium commonly shows white to pink mold, often scattered on individual kernels rather than dense blue-green masses at the tip Document 1; Document 2.
Very lowâDiplodia produces a white to gray mold that often fills the entire ear under the husk rather than a blue-green tip growth Document 1; Document 2.

diagnosis.weather_correlation: High humidity (92%) with full cloud cover fosters prolonged kernel/silk wetness; Penicillium spp. can grow at low temperatures, allowing persistence and sporulation despite the current 1.3Â°C, which matches the observed blue-green tip rot. Absence of recent precipitation suggests development on previously wetted/damaged tips or during post-harvest handling if ears were not dried quickly Document 1; Document 2; Document 3.

immediate_actions: Field-sort and remove heavily moldy ears to reduce inoculum and prevent cross-contamination during harvest and handling Document 1; Document 2.
Harvest affected areas promptly and segregate grain from cleaner fields/lots; keep moldy ears out of the primary storage stream Document 1; Document 2.

Use appropriate PPE (respirator, gloves) when handling moldy grain to avoid inhalation/allergen exposure; do not feed visibly moldy grain without mycotoxin testing as some Penicillium spp. can produce toxins Document 1; Document 2; Document 3.

treatment_options.description: Dry grain rapidly to â¤14â15% moisture and cool it quickly; maintain good aeration to keep grain cool and dry, which halts further Penicillium growth and limits spoilage.
treatment_options.effectiveness: highâDrying and cooling are the most effective post-infection measures; fungicides are not effective once ear rot is established.
treatment_options.weather_timing: Begin immediately. With outdoor air cold but very humid, use heated/controlled-air drying and continuous aeration; avoid delays while RH remains high and skies are cloudy Document 1; Document 2.

treatment_options.description: Screen and discard fines and visibly moldy kernels; keep affected lots separate from sound grain to limit spread and quality loss.
treatment_options.effectiveness: medium to highâReduces inoculum and mycotoxin risk in the main lot.
treatment_options.weather_timing: Do promptly after harvest; continue during cool, humid periods to prevent further contamination Document 1; Document 2.

preventive_measures: Select hybrids with tight husk coverage and good resistance to ear rots; manage insects and birds that damage ear tips Document 1; Document 2.
Harvest promptly at maturity, especially if humid/cloudy conditions persist, and dry/cool grain quickly Document 1; Document 2.
Sanitize combines, wagons, dryers, and storage bins before filling; remove old grain and debris to reduce Penicillium inoculum Document 1.
Adjust harvesting equipment to minimize kernel damage and fines, which favor mold growth Document 2.
recommendations.monitoring_advice: While RH stays >80% and skies remain cloudy/cold, check fields and storage daily for new mold patches, rising grain moisture, musty odors, and temperature hotspots; sample segregated lots and submit for mycotoxin testing if any mold is detected Document 1; Document 2.
weather_based_recommendations: Because humidity is 92% with 100% cloud cover, prioritize artificial/controlled-air drying now rather than waiting for ambient air to dry the grain Document 1; Document 2.

Use aeration to keep stored grain as cool as possible; cold, dry air cycles are beneficial for cooling despite high nightly humidityârun fans when outside air dew point is lower than grain temperature Document 1.
Avoid field operations that would expose more ear tips while plants are damp; schedule harvest for times with the lowest RH available (often midday) to reduce spread during handling Document 2.

sources.title: Penicillium Ear Rot of Corn
sources.author: Iowa State University Extension and Outreach

sources.title: Penicillium Ear Rot in Corn
sources.author: Ohio State University Extension (Ohioline Fact Sheet PLPATH-CER-04)

response.limitations: Diagnosis is based on visible blue-green sporulation at the ear tip and current weather favoring Penicillium growth; other ear rot fungi can co-occur and some Penicillium species produce mycotoxins. Species-level identification and toxin profile require laboratory testing; test grain before feeding or blending.
